data.title: MTY Food Group Logs 4Q Loss On Impairment Charge, FX Drag
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

MTY Food Group swung to a loss in the fourth quarter after the company booked an impairment charge on one of its brand assets as well as large foreign exchange losses.

The Canadian restaurant franchiser on Friday posted a net loss of 55.3 million Canadian dollars ($39 million), or C$2.34 a share, compared with a profit of C$16.4 million, or C$0.67 a share, in the comparable quarter a year ago.

According to FactSet, analysts expected a slight decline in profit to C$0.65 a share.

MTY Food said the decline was largely due to a C$64.6 million impairment charges on property, plant and equipment, intangibles assets and goodwill related to its Papa Murphy's brand due to lower than expected past performance and a less rosy future growth prospect.

The other drag on loss was foreign exchange losses of C$26.3 million taken primarily on intercompany loans, it said.

Adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization fell to a profit of C$58.8 million from C$60.4 million.

Revenue rose to C$284.5 million from C$280 million, beating expectations of a decline to C$273.5 million.

System sales, a key franchise industry metric that tracks total sales of all franchiser, rose to C$1.37 billion from C$1.34 billion.

MTY Food's U.S. segment achieved overall sales growth, due to positive organic growth of 2% as well as a positive effects of foreign exchange rates while Canada saw organic growth of 1% compared with prior year.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Earnings Flash (TRP) TC Energy Posts Q4 Revenue of CA$3.58B, vs. FactSet Est of CA$3.62B
Social Buzz: Wallstreetbets Stocks Mostly Higher Premarket Friday; Hims & Hers Health, Intel to Advance
data.description:
The most-talked-about stocks in the Reddit subforum Wallstreetbets were mostly higher hours before Friday's opening bell.

Hims & Hers Health advanced by 3.5% pre-bell, adding to its 27.7% rise at Thursday's close.

Intel rose by 2.3% premarket, after closing Thursday with a 7.3% increase.

Tesla was up 1.5% in pre-bell hours, following a 5.8% increase from the previous session.

Advanced Micro Devices was 0.5% higher in premarket activity, after a 0.1% increase at Thursday's close.

Nvidia was up 0.3% pre-bell, following a 3.2% increase from the previous session.

Robinhood Markets was up 0.2% premarket, after closing Thursday with a 14.1% increase.

Reddit declined by 1.2% premarket, after closing Thursday with a 5.3% fall.

Palantir Technologies was down 0.9% hours before market open, swinging from a 0.4% increase at Thursday's close.

data.title: Sterling rises to 2025 high as easing tariff fears dent dollar
data.description:
Sterling rose to its strongest level against the dollar this year on Friday, after UK GDP data earlier this week sweetened the mood around the strength of the British economy, while the dollar weakened on some relief over U.S. tariff threats.

At 1043 GMT, the pound rose 0.1% to $1.25775 against the dollar, its highest level since December 30.

Sentiment towards the UK economy was boosted after data on Thursday showed Britain's economy unexpectedly grew by 0.1% in the final quarter of last year, strengthening the pound.

"The pound was buoyed by the broad risk on mode in the market on Thursday, and the mild surprise to the upside in the latest GDP report for Q4," said Matthew Ryan, head of market strategy at Ebury.

He added that the outlook remains complicated for the economy amid flat-lining growth in the second half of last year, elevated inflation, high mortgage rates and a high tax burden.

The pound was also helped higher on Friday by a weaker dollar as traders breathed a sigh of relief that Washington did not immediately impose reciprocal tariffs, suggesting room for negotiation, while a U.S. producer price report soothed inflation concerns.

The pound was largely flat against the euro , with one euro worth 83.39 pence.

Money markets are pricing in 60 basis points of further easing this year from the Bank of England, compared with a little more than one quarter-point from the Federal Reserve and at least four quarter-point cuts from the European Central Bank.

Market watchers are looking ahead to a fourth-quarter 2024 labour report due next Tuesday for more direction on the BoE's interest rate path, after the previous report showed British pay growth remained robust at 6% in the three months to November though there were more signs of a softening jobs market. More pay growth can add to inflationary pressures.

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0646 ET - Bitcoin rises modestly as President Trump's more gradual approach to proposed tariffs provides some respite to risky assets. Trump signed a memo instructing staff to assess retaliatory tariffs on a country by country basis with a plan expected to be released in April. "Investors are expressing relief as Trump's proposed reciprocal tariffs signal a potential opening for negotiations," Tickmill Group's Patrick Munnelly says in a note. Bitcoin is "riding a wave of positivity" with speculators holding a net long position of 786 contracts that bet on the cryptocurrency rising. Bitcoin rises 0.4% to $96,919, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0607 ET - The Swedish krona would be one of the biggest beneficiaries of any progress towards a Ukraine ceasefire deal, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. The krona's valuation is cheap and it's sensitive to risk sentiment, they say. It's also supported by the potential for Swedish fiscal stimulus and the Riksbank ending its interest-rate cutting cycle. A Ukraine peace deal would provide more relief to the krona compared to the Norwegian krone where the prospect of lower gas prices could impact its terms of trade. J.P. Morgan expects EUR/SEK to fall to 11.00 in the case of a definite resolution. EUR/SEK trades flat at 11.2290, having earlier reached a 20-week low of 11.2112, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0547 ET - Base metal prices rise, with LME three-month copper up 1.6% at $9,626 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum up 1.1% at $2,637 a ton. President Trump has set out plans for so-called reciprocal tariffs on a country-by-country basis, with additional levies on top of aluminum and steel tariffs. The administration hasn't set out plans on when copper levies could be defined or implemented, says Britannia Global Markets' Neil Welsh. The move is seen as a potential opening bid for negotiation, Welsh writes, as Trump believes the trading environment is imbalanced. The tariff risk is disrupting the global copper market with copper spreads under threat, Welsh says in a note. Copper on U.S. commodities exchange Comex is up around 19% since the start of the year, compared with around 9.5% on the LME. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0458 ET - U.S. President Donald Trump has certainly stirred things up with a frenetic stream of policy announcements, but the macro outlook is reasonably settled, says Steven Bell at Columbia Threadneedle Investments in a podcast. "Growth is steady at a goldilocks pace--strong enough to keep the economy well clear of recession but not so strong to push inflation higher," the chief economist says. This week's U.S. CPI data surprised markets with a higher-than-expected print, prompting selloff in U.S. Treasurys. However, Bell says the bigger picture is of a gentle decline in U.S. inflation back towards the Federal Reserve's 2% target. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0455 ET - Friday's fall in the dollar is unlikely to be sustained as tariff uncertainty remains, MUFG Bank analyst Lee Hardman says in a note. The U.S. currency hits a two-month low against a basket of currencies on relief that President Trump's plans for reciprocal tariffs won't be implemented until at least April. However, reciprocal tariffs will be based not only on tariffs imposed by other countries on U.S. imports but also other factors such as VAT, exchange rates and regulations. "We are more wary of a more disruptive outcome now that non-tariff factors will be included," Hardman says. Trade-policy uncertainty should remain supportive for the dollar, he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.4% to a two-month low of 106.831. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0418 ET - Oil prices edge higher in early trade after U.S. President Trump delayed immediate plans to impose reciprocal tariffs, easing fears of a trade war that could hurt global growth and demand for crude. Brent is up 0.6% at $75.48 a barrel, while WTI rises 0.5% to $71.64 a barrel. Trump's decision to have federal agencies study how to adjust tariffs to match those of other countries by April 1 provided some temporary relief to the market and pushed down the U.S. dollar. Meanwhile, the IEA lifted its oil-demand growth forecast and lowered its expectations for a global oil surplus this year, providing further support to market sentiment. Still, gains continue to be capped by persistent fears of escalating trade tensions and prospects of a potential peace deal between Russia and Ukraine. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0337 ET - Heightened uncertainty over the trade tariffs U.S. President Trump could implement means investors need to ensure they have diverse portfolios and employ hedging strategies, says UBS Global Wealth Management's Mark Haefele. "While the delay in implementation limits immediate market impact, the risk of retaliation remains," the chief investment officer says. Within equities, capital preservation strategies could be necessary to prevent losses if stocks turn lower. UBS GWM favors high-grade and investment-grade bonds as a way to diversify portfolios as these offer "insulation against uncertainty." (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0332 ET - The euro could rise further on relief that President Trump's latest tariff plans aren't immediate, ING analyst Chris Turner says in a note. With tariffs to be assessed on a country-by-country basis in a report expected on April 1, trading partners have time to fine-tune strategies. However, the outcome could be "eye-wateringly large tariffs against some of the key countries with which the U.S. runs a goods deficit." The EU will "certainly be in the cross-hairs." That means the euro's gains should be limited to $1.0535-$1.0575 near-term, and it still risks falling to parity in the second quarter, he says. The euro rises to a two-and-a-half-week high of $1.0482, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0253 ET - The dollar falls to a two-month low against a basket of currencies after President Trump announced plans for reciprocal tariffs to be assessed on a country-by-country basis as opposed to blanket tariffs. Such a process should "inevitably take some time" with investigations expected to complete by April 1 and remedies implemented immediately after, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. "In the meantime, questions whether this tariff threat will be used as a negotiating tool are likely to linger." The limited tariff news and lower yields after Thursday's U.S. producer prices data dented the dollar, they say. The DXY dollar index falls to a low of 106.962. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0250 ET - U.S. Treasurys trade little changed in early European hours after President Trump ordered federal agencies on Thursday to explore how to adjust U.S. tariffs to match of those of other countries. Treasurys rallied Thursday, fully reversing Wednesday's CPI-triggered selloff. That said, Trump's tariffs measures continue to hang over markets. "The threat of those reciprocal tariffs, plus apparent upcoming announcements on further tariffs on cars, chips, and pharmaceutical products will thus hang over proceedings like the 'sword of Damocles' for a while yet," says Pepperstone's Michael Brown in a note. This creates choppy trading conditions and higher volatility, the senior research strategist says. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield last trades 2 basis points higher at 4.545%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0249 ET - The U.K.'s FTSE 100 index is expected to open slightly higher, up 0.1%, according to IG, while Germany's DAX looks set to open 0.4% lower after hitting record highs recently. The recent rally in stocks has been helped by earnings data, says XTB's Kathleen Brooks in a note. Traders have largely shrugged off tariff threats for now, but the prospect of Trump announcing tariffs against European goods still looms. President Trump ordered federal agencies on Thursday to explore how to adjust U.S. tariffs to match of those of other countries. Brooks says these could take longer to plan. France's CAC 40 is expected to open down 0.1%, according to IG. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

1752 ET - AUD/USD has strengthened this week, and now trades above 0.6300, having fallen below 0.6250 at one point, but it remains vulnerable because "peak tariff" has not been reached yet, says Joe Capurso, economist at CBA. U.S. President Trump has signaled the implementation of more tariffs on imports and even if Australia can convince the U.S. government to not impose tariffs, some Australian exports to third economies are part of the export supply chain to the U.S., he adds. CBA estimates 2.4% of Australian GDP satisfies U.S. domestic demand through direct and indirect exports, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

data.title: Bitcoin Rises on Relief Over Trump's Tariff Plans — Market Talk
data.description:
1146 GMT - Bitcoin rises modestly as President Trump's more gradual approach to proposed tariffs provides some respite to risky assets. Trump signed a memo instructing staff to assess retaliatory tariffs on a country by country basis with a plan expected to be released in April. "Investors are expressing relief as Trump's proposed reciprocal tariffs signal a potential opening for negotiations," Tickmill Group's Patrick Munnelly says in a note. Bitcoin is "riding a wave of positivity" with speculators holding a net long position of 786 contracts that bet on the cryptocurrency rising. Bitcoin rises 0.4% to $96,919, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1134 GMT - Demand for U.K. government bonds has been strong in the first quarter of 2025 despite increased gilt issuance by the Debt Management Office, Tickmill Group's Patrick Munelly says in a note. This has limited any negative impact on gilts, he says. "Overall, the market's appetite has been sufficient to absorb the increased [gilt] supply, mitigating any significant adverse effects on the secondary market," he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1133 GMT - Sterling could fall if any potential deal to end the Ukraine-Russia conflict resulted in more aggressive interest-rate cuts from the Bank of England, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. Weak U.K. growth already suggests the BOE should accelerate rate cuts but inflation could ease if gas prices decline on any peace deal. If inflation falls, the market could price in more rate cuts, weakening sterling. The prospect of more considerable rate cuts would more than offset the positive impact of improved risk sentiment for sterling. "Furthermore, any increase in regional yields in response to possibility of more defence spending could also be problematic for sterling given its vulnerability given limited fiscal room." (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1128 GMT - The Russian ruble drops after Russia's central bank held interest rates at 21%, as anticipated. The central bank said inflationary pressures remain elevated but should gradually decline in the coming months as lending activity cools and household savings rise. "We think that interest rates are likely to remain on hold until the second half of this year, and that monetary loosening will be more limited than most others expect," Capital Economics economist Nicholas Farr says in a note. If the Ukraine war ended soon, that could increase the scope for rate cuts down the line, he says. USD/RUB rises more than 2% on the day to 91.4504 after the decision, from 90.5001 beforehand. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1107 GMT - The Swedish krona would be one of the biggest beneficiaries of any progress towards a Ukraine ceasefire deal, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. The krona's valuation is cheap and it's sensitive to risk sentiment, they say. It's also supported by the potential for Swedish fiscal stimulus and the Riksbank ending its interest-rate cutting cycle. A Ukraine peace deal would provide more relief to the krona compared to the Norwegian krone where the prospect of lower gas prices could impact its terms of trade. J.P. Morgan expects EUR/SEK to fall to 11.00 in the case of a definite resolution. EUR/SEK trades flat at 11.2290, having earlier reached a 20-week low of 11.2112, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1030 GMT - Eurozone growth of 0.1% is hardly anything for Europe's policymakers to get excited about, capital Economics' Jack-Allen Reynolds says. The eurozone economy expanded by that slight amount in last three months of 2024, according to fresh estimates Friday that bump up a previous estimation of stagnation across the 20 nations that use the euro. That level of growth is still very weak and the signs are for continued sluggishness at the start of 2025, Allen-Reynolds says. "There is also little evidence of a turnaround in the region's woeful productivity performance," he tells investors in a note. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0958 GMT - U.S. President Donald Trump has certainly stirred things up with a frenetic stream of policy announcements, but the macro outlook is reasonably settled, says Steven Bell at Columbia Threadneedle Investments in a podcast. "Growth is steady at a goldilocks pace--strong enough to keep the economy well clear of recession but not so strong to push inflation higher," the chief economist says. This week's U.S. CPI data surprised markets with a higher-than-expected print, prompting selloff in U.S. Treasurys. However, Bell says the bigger picture is of a gentle decline in U.S. inflation back towards the Federal Reserve's 2% target. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0955 GMT - Friday's fall in the dollar is unlikely to be sustained as tariff uncertainty remains, MUFG Bank analyst Lee Hardman says in a note. The U.S. currency hits a two-month low against a basket of currencies on relief that President Trump's plans for reciprocal tariffs won't be implemented until at least April. However, reciprocal tariffs will be based not only on tariffs imposed by other countries on U.S. imports but also other factors such as VAT, exchange rates and regulations. "We are more wary of a more disruptive outcome now that non-tariff factors will be included," Hardman says. Trade-policy uncertainty should remain supportive for the dollar, he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.4% to a two-month low of 106.831. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0954 GMT - The outcome of the Modi-Trump meeting aligns with expectations, with near-term trade frictions set to persist, but a strategic partnership possible over the medium term, according to Nomura analysts. Discussions centered around a trade deal, India increasing energy imports from the U.S., defense cooperation and a reiteration of the strategic partnership, Sonal Varma and Aurodeep Nandi write in a report. Negotiations on a U.S.-India trade deal will begin, with some concessions likely, they say. Talks are expected to focus on India purchasing more U.S. oil & gas, renewable energy and defense equipment, they add. "But given India's higher tariff rate, high non-tariff barriers and its trade surplus with the U.S., it remains in Trump's line of fire on reciprocal tariffs," they write. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0940 GMT - China's latest credit and lending data suggests policymakers are still struggling to provide much of a lift to broad credit growth, Capital Economics says. China economist Zichun Huang attributes the January headline figures showing new local-currency loans at record highs to the usual seasonal pattern, noting that net lending is always strongest at the start of the year. To cut through the seasonality, CE looks at the data in terms of on-year growth in credit outstanding. On that basis, bank lending slowed from December to a fresh low last month, Huang writes. In the coming quarters, she thinks robust government bond issuance can lend support, but weak private demand will likely keep credit growth subdued. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0938 GMT - The Russian central bank is expected to hold interest rates at 21% in a decision at 1030 GMT as the ruble's recent recovery has likely helped tackle inflation, Commerzbank analyst Tatha Ghose says in a note. The currency's weakness after the last round of U.S. sanctions on Russia meant the central bank had to use large rate rises to counter the inflation pass-through, he says. The ruble has recovered substantially since then for "reasons not entirely clear," given USD/RUB and EUR/RUB are technical fixes that don't trade like normal floating exchange rates. However, cautious optimism over a Ukraine ceasefire deal could have offered some support. USD/RUB rises 0.4% to 89.8250 after hitting a five-month low of 89.2564 Thursday, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0918 GMT - Oil prices edge higher in early trade after U.S. President Trump delayed immediate plans to impose reciprocal tariffs, easing fears of a trade war that could hurt global growth and demand for crude. Brent is up 0.6% at $75.48 a barrel, while WTI rises 0.5% to $71.64 a barrel. Trump's decision to have federal agencies study how to adjust tariffs to match those of other countries by April 1 provided some temporary relief to the market and pushed down the U.S. dollar. Meanwhile, the IEA lifted its oil-demand growth forecast and lowered its expectations for a global oil surplus this year, providing further support to market sentiment. Still, gains continue to be capped by persistent fears of escalating trade tensions and prospects of a potential peace deal between Russia and Ukraine. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

Citigroup Adjusts Price Target on eBay to $80 From $75, Maintains Buy Rating

data.title: European Miners to Report Results Under Cloud of U.S. Tariffs, Global Trade War — Sector Preview
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker

European miners are scheduled to report fourth-quarter and full-year results over the next four weeks. Gold prices are trading near record highs as investors turn to safe haven assets as economic and geopolitical uncertainty intensifies. Investors are still trying to separate President Trump's trade tariff bluster from actual trade policy, while sluggish economic growth in China further clouds the outlook for miners. Here is what you need to know.

During the fourth-quarter, the STOXX Europe 600 Basic Resources index fell 10%, while the broader Stoxx Europe 600 fell 2.9%

Meanwhile, over the past three months, Antofagasta shares have risen 16% while Anglo American's are up 12%. Shares in diversified mining giant Glencore are down 4.9%.

WHAT TO WATCH

  • Gold prices ended the quarter at $2,625 an ounce--a 27% increase over the year, RBC Capital Markets analysts wrote. Gold prices are now testing record highs while silver is rallying hard, underpinned by geopolitical uncertainty and concerns around weak global economic growth, Berenberg analysts wrote. Prices for precious metals are expected to continue rising, boosting earnings and shareholder value, they added.
  • Uncertainty around future U.S. tariffs and a global trade war, combined with questions over the trajectory of China's economic recovery, cloud the price outlook for commodities, Citi analysts wrote in a research note. Antofagasta would be particularly vulnerable should a trade war trigger falling copper prices, given it is hitting peak capex spend on its $4.4 billion second Centinela concentrator, RBC analysts wrote.
  • Investors will be scrutinizing capital expenditure on projects that don't deliver material near-term volume growth, Citi analysts wrote in a recent research note. They will reward companies that show greater capital discipline, with Glencore and Anglo American their preferred choices. The analysts do not expect material headwinds from cost inflation in 2025 but expect capex to trend higher across the sector.
  • Market watchers will be looking for comments from miners on the outlook for copper demand and production. COMEX copper trades around $4.769 a pound but RBC analysts last month said they expect it to trade lower at around $4.00 a pound over 2025. The analysts remain constructive on the long-term outlook for copper due to energy transition demands and future supply constraints.

WHEN COMPANIES ARE SCHEDULED TO REPORT:

  • Anglo American Platinum: Feb. 17
  • Antofagasta: Feb. 18
  • Rio Tinto: Feb. 19
  • Glencore: Feb. 19
  • AngloGold Ashanti: Feb. 19
  • Anglo American: Feb. 20
  • Sibanye-Stillwater: Feb. 21
  • Fresnillo: March. 4
  • Harmony Gold: March. 4
  • Endeavour Mining: March. 6
  • Hochschild Mining: March. 12

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: Applied Materials Issues Downbeat Quarterly Revenue Outlook as China Trade Restrictions Weigh
data.description:
Applied Materials shares fell early Friday as the company issued a downbeat fiscal second-quarter revenue outlook at the midpoint amid the impact of US restrictions on exports to China, while the semiconductor equipment manufacturer recorded better-than-expected results in the preceding three-month period.

The company anticipates revenue to come in at $7.1 billion, plus or minus $400 million, for the ongoing quarter, it said late Thursday. The current consensus on FactSet is for sales of $7.16 billion. Adjusted earnings are pegged at $2.30 a share for the second quarter, plus or minus $0.18, while the Street is looking for $2.30.

"The ability of US companies to serve the China market is constrained and has been further limited by updated trade rules announced in December and January," Chief Executive Gary Dickerson said during an earnings call, according to a FactSet transcript. The company expects to face a revenue headwind of about $400 million in fiscal 2025 as a result of the expanded export controls, with "nearly half" of the impact to be incurred in the second quarter, Chief Financial Officer Brice Hill said on the call.

Earlier in February, US President Donald Trump imposed a 10% levy on imports from China, which announced its own retaliatory tariffs against the country.

China was Applied Materials' largest market by revenue share in the first quarter, but saw its contribution drop to 31% of overall revenue from 45%in the prior-year quarter. "For (the second quarter) we expect that China as a percentage of total revenue will be about 5 percentage points lower than in (the first quarter)," Hill said. "This is below the normalized level of approximately 30%."

The stock declined 5% in premarket activity.

The impact of trade restrictions is expected to be "more weighted" to the company's applied global services segment in the second half of the fiscal year, Hill told analysts. The company expects the division to return to growth in the third quarter, the CFO added.

"While our near-term service growth is negatively impacted by trade restrictions, we remain confident that we will still grow (applied global services) at a low-double digit annualized growth rate over the longer term," Dickerson said on the call.

For the three months through Jan. 26, adjusted EPS increased 12% year over year to $2.38, topping the average analyst estimate of $2.28. Revenue improved to $7.17 billion from $6.71 billion last year, ahead of the market's forecast of $7.15 billion.

Sales in the semiconductor systems segment rose to $5.36 billion from $4.91 billion, while applied global services advanced to $1.59 billion from $1.48 billion. Display revenue declined to $183 million from $244 million last year, according to the company.

data.title: Ukraine Peace Prospects Keep European Bourses Near Record Highs
data.description:
European bourses were mixed and muted midday Friday as traders weighed Ukraine peace initiatives and awaited fresh catalysts, leaving broad-market equity indices near record highs.

Tech stocks led gainers, while food issues lagged.

Investors also eyed Wall Street futures mildly signaling red and choppy closes overnight on Asian exchanges, although Hong Kong gained 3.7% led by a rally in tech shares.

In economic news, Q4 gross domestic product (GDP) rose 0.9% on the year in the euro area, and by 1.1% in the broader European Union, Eurostat reported.

The pan-continental Stoxx Europe 600 Index was off 0.1% mid-session.

The Stoxx Europe 600 Technology Index was up 0.7%, and the Stoxx 600 Banks Index gained 0.1%.

The Stoxx Europe 600 Oil and Gas Index was up 0.4%, but the Stoxx 600 Europe Food and Beverage Index declined 0.2%.

The REITE, a European REIT index, rose 0.3%, and the Stoxx Europe 600 Retail Index inclined 0.4%.

On the national market indexes, Germany's DAX was off 0.3%, and the FTSE 100 in London was down 0.2%. The CAC 40 in Paris was off 0.3%, and Spain's IBEX 35 was flat.

Yields on benchmark 10-year German bonds were steady, near 2.42%.

Front-month North Sea Brent crude oil futures were stable near $75.08 per barrel.

The Euro Stoxx 50 volatility index was down 1.7% to 16.24, indicating below-average volatility for European stock markets in the next 30 days, a positive signal. A reading above 20 indicates choppier markets ahead, while below 20 suggests calmer exchanges.

data.title: Russian Stocks Surge to 9-Month High
data.description:
The MOEX Russia index surged to the 3,220 mark in February, its highest since May of last year and in line with the strong momentum for other Russian assets amid expectations that Russia’s war with Ukraine may be approaching its end.

US President Trump held a call with Russian President Putin and signaled that the US will mediate talks for a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine in their war.

Additionally, the lenient rhetoric with concessions for Russia by US authorities backed expectations that Washington may remove sanctions and reintroduce Russian capital markets into the global financial system, which would raise exports and trigger an aggressive inflow of foreign funds into the Moscow Exchange.

Gazprom rallied over 70% since testing record lows in November of last year, while Sberbank, Rosneft, and Lukoil also traded within 6-month highs.

On the monetary policy front, the Bank of Russia maintained its key rate at 21% but signaled the possibility of a rate hike next month.

data.title: Fortis Quarterly Earnings Lifted by Rate Base Growth
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

Fortis logged a rise in earnings for the latest quarter, thanks to growth in the Canadian utility's rate base and new customer rates at its Central Hudson electric utility that kicked in from July.

The holding company, which has electricity and natural gas operations in North America and the Caribbean, recorded an increase in fourth-quarter net earnings to 396 million Canadian dollars ($279 million), or C$0.79 a share, from C$381 million, or C$0.78, a year earlier.

On an adjusted basis to excluding items Fortis's management excludes in key decision making, per-share earnings came in at C$0.83, ahead of the C$0.81 mean forecast of analysts polled by FactSet.

New customer rates at Central Hudson shifted the timing of quarterly rate recovery to related costs, which Fortis said resulted in higher revenue and earnings in the fourth quarter.

That was tempered by a refund liability recognized at its ITC electricity transmission business in the U.S., unrealized losses on derivative contracts, and prior-year gain on the sale of the Aitken Creek gas storage facilities in British Columbia in 2023. Fortis also saw lower earnings in Arizona, driven by higher operating expenses.

For the full year, earnings increased to C$1.83 billion from C$1.71 billion in 2023, while revenue slipped slightly to C$11.51 billion from C$11.52 billion.

The St. John's, Newfoundland, utility's capital expenditures for 2024 totaled C$5.2 billion. The company is targeting a capital plan of C$26 billion over 2025 to 2029, which aims to support annual dividend growth 4% to 6% over that period.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: Update: Market Chatter: Telefónica Said to Tap JPMorgan for Mexican Unit Sale
data.description:
(Updates to add JPMorgan's statement)

Telefónica appointed JPMorgan to divest its Mexican business, targeting to carry out the deal before its annual shareholder meeting usually held in April or May, Spanish newspaper Cinco Días reported Friday, citing sources.

JPMorgan declined while the Spanish telco did not immediately respond to a request for comment from MT Newswires.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: India stocks, rupee, swaps, call at close
data.description:
STOCKS:

The benchmark BSE Sensex was down 0.26% at 75,939.21, while the broader NSE Nifty 50 index declined 0.44% to 22,929.25, as investors fretted over the implications of U.S. President Donald Trump's plans to impose reciprocal tariffs, which analysts said could hurt the country the most among its Asian peers.

RUPEE:

The Indian rupee rose 0.1% versus the U.S. dollar to 86.8225, mirroring gains in most regional currencies on relief that U.S. President Donald Trump's reciprocal tariff plan will not be implemented immediately.

GOVERNMENT BONDS:

The benchmark 10-year bond (IN067934G=CC) was quoted at 100.57 rupees, with the yield little changed at 6.7071%, as the weekly debt auction, which included the benchmark paper, came in largely along expected lines.

OVERNIGHT INDEX SWAPS:

The one-year overnight index swap rate (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) was down over 2 bps at 6.3%, while the five-year swap rate (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) dropped over 4 bps to 6.0925%.

CALL MONEY:

India's overnight call money rate (INROND=) was at 5.80% and the overnight TREPS rate (INTREPTOT=TCCL) was at 6.25%.

data.title: European Commission OKs Novo Holdings, TA Associates' Purchase of Biocomposites
data.description:
The European Commission cleared the acquisition of Matrix Topco, d/b/a Biocomposites, by Novo Holdings, the controlling shareholder of Danish pharma giant Novo Nordisk (NOVO-B.CO), and US private equity company TA Associates Management.

The UK-based healthcare company mainly develops calcium compounds and specialty polymer products for surgical use, according to a Friday release.

The commission concluded that the transaction would not raise competition concerns because the joint venture has negligible activities in the European Economic Area and the companies will have limited market positions after it closes.

data.title: NeuroPace Announces Pricing of Public Offering of $65 Million of Common Stock
data.description:
MOUNTAIN VIEW, Calif., Feb. 14, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — NeuroPace, Inc. today announced the pricing of an underwritten public offering of 6,500,000 shares of its common stock at a public offering price of $10.00 per share. All of the shares are being sold by NeuroPace. The aggregate gross proceeds from the offering are expected to be $65 million, before deducting the underwriting discounts and commissions and offering expenses. The offering is expected to close on February 18, 2025, subject to customary closing conditions. In addition, NeuroPace has granted the underwriters a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional 975,000 shares of its common stock at the public offering price.

NeuroPace expects to use approximately $49.5 million of the net proceeds from the offering to repurchase 5,270,845 shares held by NeuroPace’s significant stockholder, KCK Ltd., at $9.40 per share, which is the price per share at which the underwriters will purchase shares of NeuroPace’s common stock from the company in the public offering. NeuroPace intends to use the remaining net proceeds from the offering, together with any net proceeds received from the underwriters’ option, for general corporate purposes, which may include clinical trial and other research and development expenses, selling, general and administrative expenses, debt reduction and working capital.

J.P. Morgan, Cantor, Wells Fargo Securities and Leerink Partners are acting as joint book-running managers for the offering and Lake Street Capital Markets is acting as lead manager for the offering.

The shares of common stock described above are being offered by NeuroPace pursuant to a shelf registration statement filed by NeuroPace with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) that was declared effective by the SEC on November 22, 2022. The offering is being made only by means of a prospectus and prospectus supplement that form a part of the registration statement. A preliminary prospectus supplement and accompanying prospectus relating to the offering were filed with the SEC and are available on the SEC’s website located at http://www.sec.gov. A final prospectus supplement and accompanying prospectus relating to the offering will be filed with the SEC and will be available on the SEC’s website located at http://www.sec.gov. Copies of the final prospectus supplement and the accompanying prospectus related to this offering, when available, may be obtained from J.P. Morgan Securities LLC, c/o Broadridge Financial Solutions, 1155 Long Island Avenue, Edgewood, New York 11717, or email: prospectus-eq_fi@jpmchase.com and postsalemanualrequests@broadridge.com; Cantor Fitzgerald & Co., Attn: Capital Markets, 110 East 59th Street, 6th Floor, New York, New York, 10022, or email: prospectus@cantor.com; Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, 90 South 7th Street, 5th Floor, Minneapolis, Minnesota 55402, at 800-645-3751 (option #5) or email a request to WFScustomerservice@wellsfargo.com; Leerink Partners LLC, Syndicate Department, 53 State Street, 40th Floor, Boston, Massachusetts 02109, by telephone at (800) 808-7525 ext. 6105, or by email at syndicate@leerink.com; or Lake Street Capital Markets, LLC, 920 Second Avenue South, Suite 700, Minneapolis, Minnesota 55402, telephone: (612) 326-1305, or email: prospectus@lakestreetcm.com.

This press release shall not constitute an offer to sell or the solicitation of an offer to buy these securities, nor shall there be any sale of these securities in any state or other jurisdiction in which such offer, solicitation or sale would be unlawful prior to the registration or qualification under the securities laws of any such state or other jurisdiction.

About NeuroPace

Based in Mountain View, Calif., NeuroPace is a medical device company focused on transforming the lives of people living with epilepsy by reducing or eliminating the occurrence of debilitating seizures. Its novel and differentiated RNS System is the first and only commercially available, brain-responsive platform that delivers personalized, real-time treatment at the seizure source. This platform can drive a better standard of care for patients living with drug-resistant epilepsy and has the potential to offer a more personalized solution and improved outcomes to the large population of patients suffering from other brain disorders.

Forward Looking Statements

Certain statements in this press release are forward-looking statements that involve a number of risks and uncertainties. These statements may be identified by introductory words such as “anticipate,” “believe,” “expects,” “intends,” “may,” “plan,” “should,” “subject to,” “will,” “would” or words of similar meaning, or by the fact that they do not relate strictly to historical or current facts. Such forward-looking statements include statements regarding the timing and completion of the offering and the satisfaction of customary closing conditions related to the offering, the anticipated total gross proceeds from the offering and the planned use of the net proceeds of the offering, including those received from the exercise of the underwriters’ option. For such statements, NeuroPace claims the protection of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. Actual events or results may differ materially from NeuroPace’s expectations. Factors that could cause actual results to differ materially from the forward-looking statements include, but are not limited to, risks and uncertainties associated with market conditions and the satisfaction of customary closing conditions related to the proposed offering, and those factors disclosed in NeuroPace’s filings with the SEC, including its Quarterly Report on Form 10-Q for the quarter ended September 30, 2024. These forward-looking statements represent NeuroPace’s judgment as of the time of this release. NeuroPace disclaims any intent or obligation to update these forward-looking statements, other than as may be required under applicable law.

Jeremy Feffer

Managing Director

data.title: InflaRx falls after pricing public offering
data.description:
** U.S.-listed shares of German-based biopharma firm InflaRx , fall 18.5% to $2.20 premarket

** On Thursday, co said it is raising $30 mln through public offering

** Co is selling 8.25 mln shares at $2/shr; represents a 25.9% discount to the stock's last close

** Also selling pre-funded warrants to buy up to 6.75 mln more shares to certain investors, which can be exercised for $0.001/shr

** Co intends to use the net proceeds to develop new medicines, including vilobelimab and INF904, and for general company purposes

** Co said it may sell up to 2.25 mln more shares if the underwriters want to buy them

** Co has 58.9 mln outstanding shares with a market cap of ~$159 mln - data compiled from LSEG

** Guggenheim Securities, LLC acting as book-running manager for the offering

** Co gained 50.6% in 2024
